dimensions.name: Query Construction
dimensions.summary: Ability to write correct, readable, and efficient SQL.
dimensions.definition: The ability to write correct, readable SQL that accurately expresses data retrieval and manipulation intent.
dimensions.whyItMatters: Query Construction is the foundational skill. All other dimensions build on the ability to express intent precisely in SQL.
assessmentQuestions: Do you use CTEs rather than deeply nested subqueries?
Can you write a correct query when NULLs are present in join columns?
Do you choose JOIN types deliberately based on the data relationship?

dimensions.name: Data Modeling
dimensions.summary: Skill in designing relational schemas and entity relationships.
dimensions.definition: Skill in designing relational schemas: normalization, entity relationships, constraints, and structural integrity.
dimensions.whyItMatters: Schema design decisions are expensive to reverse. A poorly modeled schema constrains every query and integration built on top of it.
assessmentQuestions: Can you identify and resolve a second normal form violation?
Do you add foreign key constraints as a default practice?
Can you explain when denormalization is the right choice?

dimensions.name: Analytical Techniques
dimensions.summary: Proficiency with aggregation, window functions, and analysis patterns.
dimensions.definition: Proficiency with aggregation patterns, window functions, CTEs, pivoting, and advanced data analysis constructs.
dimensions.whyItMatters: Analytical Techniques determines the range of questions a practitioner can answer directly in SQL without resorting to application code.
assessmentQuestions: Can you write a rolling 7-day average using a window function?
Can you compute a retention cohort table in SQL?
Do you know when to use FILTER (WHERE ...) vs. a CASE expression inside an aggregate?

dimensions.name: Performance & Optimization
dimensions.summary: Understanding of index design, execution plans, and query tuning.
dimensions.definition: Understanding of index design, execution plan analysis, query tuning, and database statistics.
dimensions.whyItMatters: Queries that work correctly at 1,000 rows may fail at 10,000,000. Performance separates practitioners who can work on production systems from those who cannot.
assessmentQuestions: Can you read an execution plan and identify why a query performs a sequential scan?
Do you understand how cardinality estimates affect join order selection?
Can you design a composite index for a specific query's filter and sort requirements?

dimensions.name: Data Integrity & Transactions
dimensions.summary: Command of constraints, ACID properties, and transactional design.
dimensions.definition: Command of constraints, ACID properties, isolation levels, and transactional design.
dimensions.whyItMatters: A database that produces fast but inconsistent results is worse than a slow one. Data Integrity determines whether a system can be trusted under concurrent load.
assessmentQuestions: Can you explain the difference between READ COMMITTED and REPEATABLE READ with a concrete scenario?
Do you understand the locking implications of adding a foreign key constraint on a large table?
Can you identify when optimistic locking is preferable to pessimistic locking?

dimensions.name: Tooling & Workflow
dimensions.summary: Use of SQL clients, migrations, version control, and CI/CD.
dimensions.definition: Use of SQL clients, schema migration tools, version control for database changes, automated testing, and CI/CD integration.
dimensions.whyItMatters: SQL written outside of a version-controlled, reviewable workflow is a liability. Tooling & Workflow makes database changes auditable and safe at team scale.
assessmentQuestions: Do all schema changes go through a migration tool?
Are your migrations tested in CI before reaching production?
Do you use a SQL linter or formatter as part of your review process?

dimensions.name: Collaboration & Standards
dimensions.summary: Adherence to naming conventions, documentation, and data governance.
dimensions.definition: Adherence to naming conventions, schema documentation, peer review of database changes, and cross-team data contracts.
dimensions.whyItMatters: Schemas outlive the teams that build them. Without consistent standards, database complexity compounds until it becomes a systemic organizational constraint.
assessmentQuestions: Do you follow a consistent naming convention and apply it to every object?
Do schema changes go through peer review before production?
Can you describe what a data contract is and when you would use one?
